description: Exelon is a U.S. regulated utility holding company delivering electric transmission and distribution service, plus natural gas distribution in selected territories, through six local utility subsidiaries.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: Exelon is a regulated utility company focused on energy delivery rather than competitive power generation. Its operating companies serve customers through electric transmission and distribution networks, with natural gas distribution also present in parts of Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Delaware.

The company’s local brands include Atlantic City Electric, BGE, ComEd, Delmarva Power, PECO, and Pepco. The corporate center is therefore less a product studio than a very large collection of wires, meters, rate cases, storm plans, and public-service obligations, which is less glamorous than software but considerably harder to opt out of.

overview.title: Registry Relevance
paragraphs: For Free The World, Exelon should be mapped primarily around essential infrastructure services: electric utility service and natural gas utility service. These are monopoly or near-monopoly regulated services, so the relevant comparison space is not consumer substitution in the ordinary sense but public alternatives, municipal utilities, cooperatives, grid decentralization, efficiency, resilience, and customer data portability.

The company’s 2022 separation from Constellation is important context. Exelon is now best understood as a transmission and distribution utility platform, while generation and competitive energy supply sit outside the current Exelon perimeter.

overview.title: Research Notes
paragraphs: Future structured sync should distinguish Exelon corporate from each operating utility because service territories, regulators, customer classes, gas exposure, and grid-modernization programs vary by subsidiary. A single Exelon-level entry is useful for ownership and strategy, but product-level claims should be tied to the relevant utility where possible.

Priority evidence should come from Exelon’s investor materials, annual reports, utility subsidiary pages, state regulator filings, and FERC or SEC documents. Market-cap pages can support index and sizing metadata, but they should not carry operational claims.

sourceSuggestions.title: Exelon Corporate Profile
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Official summary of Exelon’s regulated utility model, customer scale, and subsidiary structure.

sourceSuggestions.title: Exelon Family of Companies

sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Recent official operating and financial update with business-unit detail.

sourceSuggestions.title: Exelon and Constellation Separation Announcement
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Clarifies why Exelon should be treated as a transmission and distribution utility rather than a generation-heavy energy company.
